2: Life Cycle Analysis (LCA), or more commonly known as Life Cycle Assessment, is a flexible and comprehensive methodology used to assess the environmental effects associated with a product and to carry out a cradle-to-grave assessment to analyze the energy re- quirements and the environmental impacts coordinated with all the stages of the lifetime of a product from the mining processes to extract the raw materials through the processing of these raw materials, industrialization, supply, utilization, disposal, recycling and re-use. Engineers use this methodology to support in assessing the products. LCA consists of the subsequent procedures: 1) objective and framework definition; 2) inventorying the data; 3)

impact analysis; and 4) results interpretation [1-5]. Additionally, LCA a comprehensive method used for scrutinizing and evaluating the environmental impacts of novel techniques, technologies and products. Hence, LCA can be used as a mean for approximating the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions of the different procedures in the framework of a production cycle [6,7]. Biogas technology is a prevailing method to recycle animal manure to yield fuel (biogas) and fertilizer throughout the anaer- obic digestion process [8,9]. Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a powerful emission mitigation strategy to decrease the negative environ- mental impacts of the management of livestock manure. Compared to other emissions abatement techniques such as direct field application of manure and composting, anaerobic digestion holds substantial advantages such as producing biofuel, recycling slurry and agricultural wastes, and producing fertilizer [1,10,11]. Conse- quently, LCA can be conducted to assess the environmental impacts and energy balance of biogas production [12]. Several studies car- ried out LCA in the area of biogas production to accomplish different goals, such as: (1) Analysis for specifying the appropriate

timing to install biogas plants and units [13], (2) Assessment of different sizes of biogas plants [1,14,15], (3) Environmental assess- ment of biogas plants [4,14,16-19], (4) Economic analysis of power generation [5,18], (5) Analysis of diverse biogas purification and upgrading techniques [20,21], (6) Assessment of biogas production from different substrates [7,12,22,23], (7) Assessment of resulting digestate [15,24], and (8) Investigation of the environmental impact of some biogas production stimulating technologies [25].

Nanotechnology focuses on the synthesis, characterization, design, applications and manipulation of the matter at low levels, including nanoparticles which are aggregates of metal atoms where at least one dimension has a size from two to several tens of nanometers. Reducing the size from the bulk material to nano- particles displays a scaling behavior in physical properties in the later ones, due to the large surface-to-volume portion [26].

Recently, the addition of different environmentally-friendly nanoparticles (NPs) with specific physicochemical properties such as high surface area, high reactivity, and high specificity improved the performance and the rate of anaerobic digestion [27-29]. Several studies [30-33] used nanomaterials in the form of nano- particles (NPs) of nickel (Ni), cobalt (Co), iron (Fe) and iron oxide (Fe3O4) as additives to the anaerobic digestion of slurry which increased the biogas production, reduced the lag phase, and shortened the required time to attain the highest biogas, and the photocatalytic effects were studied further [34]; [32,35-37].

According to the above-mentioned issues, several questions arise: (1) is this an environmentally-friendly technique? (2) which metal nanoparticle is the furthermost environmentally-friendly in comparison with the other metal nanoparticles? (3) what is the environmental performance of these nanoparticles? and (4) more numerous questions concerning the energy consumption in com- parison with the increased energy production by this technique, and more about the materials inputs and outputs. Accordingly, the objective of this investigation is to carry out an environmental impact assessment of the implementation of nanomaterials in biogas production from livestock manure through the anaerobic digestion process.

6: The aim of this study was to present the specific impacts on different environmental indicators mitigation of the use of nano- materials in biogas production from anaerobic digestion of live- stock manure.
7: <header>2.2. Description of biogas production and energy generation </header>

The quantity of the produced biogas and the amount of the generated energy were computed for the different scenarios/vari- ants, where these scenarios vary from each other according to the type of nanoparticles used (nickel nanoparticles, cobalt nano- particles, iron nanoparticles, and iron oxide nanoparticles). Accordingly, the results of [27,30,31] regarding the implementation of nanoparticles in the anaerobic digestion of livestock manure were further used in the calculations conducted in this research and were computed on yearly basis. For each type of nanoparticles, a biogas production estimation was conducted based on the spec- ifications [38] of standard biogas plants in Europe.
8: To study the human toxicity potential, resource depletion, ozone layer depletion potential, eutrophication and acidification as well as

to compute the greenhouse gas emissions of electricity production from biogas, the standard technique of LCA was applied using the GaBi® 6.0 tool (thinkstep AG, Germany). The beneficial energy (1 MJ electricity) was considered as functional unit. The system boundary includes the following processes: (1) supplement of feedstock, (2) biogas production, and (3) biogas utilization. Animal production and nanoparticles production were outside the system boundary. Advances which were needed for the process such as electricity were included in the system boundary (Fig. 1).
9: Regarding the biogas production systems, the life cycle in- ventory (LCI) was gathered fromprimary data acquired from the following references: [27,30,31,48-51] and research group BEK (2016) [47]. In order to characterize the impact of the biogas sys- items on global warming, the following greenhouse gases carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) were considered and converted using the CO2 equivalence factors (mass basis) for a 100 year time horizon with a global warming potential (GWP) of 25 for CH4 and 298 for N20.

Dependent on the values of biogas produced throughout each variant, the overall energy, heat energy and electrical energy were further computed. Further environmental indicators were investi- gated, which are: acidification, eutrophication, resource depletion, ozone layer depletion potential, and human toxicity potential from electricity production through different biogas production sce- narios. The electricity was considered to be generated from the biogas produced through different scenarios which were: (1) biogas produced from manure and this is the control, (2) biogas produced from manure treated with nickel nanoparticles, (3) biogas produced from manure treated with cobalt nanoparticles, (4) biogas produced from manure treated with iron nanoparticles, and (5) biogas produced from manure treated with iron oxide nanoparticles.

11: In this study, the exergetic allocation method was implemented, where exergy is defined as that part of a system's energy content which can be transformed into mechanical work [40]. Electricity is 100% exergy, therefore if exergetic allocation is performed, elec- tricity shall bear the main part of the system's GHG emissions whereas the emission share allocated to heat will remain small (Equations (1)-(3)) after [39]:

13: The statistical analysis in this study was carried out by SPPSS (version 22), using one-way ANOVA at p < 0.05 for the mean values of different elements of investigation which affect the environ- mental indicator. The significant values were divided to 5 cate- gories in letters "a, b, C, d and e", since letter "a" indicates for the best scenario that gives the lowest effect on the environmental indicator and letter "e" indicates for the worst scenario that gives the highest effect on the environmental indicator.
14: The results of energy generation and biogas production are presented in Table 1 which shows the computed values of the overall energy, heat energy and electrical energy generated from biogas produced through different variants based on the addition of the different nanoparticles. The calculations were conducted for the standard European biogas plant, where its capacity is 1400 m³ with slurry flow of 10,000 m³ per year [38]. Furthermore, the energy
content of biogas was considered as 6.095 kWh m [41]. Addi- tionally, values of dry matter content, organic dry matter content, methane content and methane production are correspondingly presented.

The mean values of different elements of investigation which affect the Acidification Potential from electricity production in kg SO2 Equiv./MJ were calculated for the different scenarios and pre- sented in Table 5. Nanoparticles (NPs) can interact with biological systems by adsorption and desorption, either as released ions, transformed single nanoparticles, agglomerates or complexes with dissolved organic matter (DOM). DOM could promote the dissolution of ENMs, but on the other hand DOM decreases the bioavailability of the dissolved ions by complexation [44]. DOM/NPs complexes are binding with metal binding receptors on the cell membrane to intracellular pathways. Methanogens actively transport Ni and Co using ATP-dependent uptake systems in order to fulfill enzymatic requirements [42,45]. The direct uptake of the single NPs via ATP- dependent uptake system depends on NPs size and speciation. NPs can be integrated in metalloenzyme, electron transfer and reduction pathways with higher binding affinity [44,46].

The results of this study show that the biogas produced from manure (standard) was the worst case in all environmental in- dicators through all scenarios. The results show that the use of cobalt nanoparticles causes the lowest greenhouse gas emissions, the lowest eutrophication, the lowest acidification and the lowest human toxicity potential among all other additives. On the other hand, the results show that the use of nickel nanoparticles causes the lowest ozone layer depletion potential and the lowest resource depletion fossil from electricity production among all other addi- tives. However [27,31], recommended the use of nickel nano- particles over cobalt nanoparticles and all other variants due to the fact that the use of nickel nanoparticles increases the production of biogas and methane in rates higher than those of cobalt nano- particles. This contradiction is caused by the conflict between en- ergy and environment. In other words, the conflicts raised by contradictory interests governed are higher biogas production which means higher energy generation on one hand versus cleaner production and environmentally-friendly processes on the other hand. An important observation is that the greenhouse gas emis- sions from the electricity generated using biogas produced without any additives, i.e. without nanoparticles, were the highest among all other variants/scenarios. Based on the results, the different variants/scenarios can be arranged according to their greenhouse gas emissions from the lowest to the highest as follows: (1) cobalt nanoparticles, (2) nickel nanoparticles, (3) iron oxide nanoparticles, (4) iron nanoparticles, and (5) the control where no additives were used.

In this research, the environmental impact was assessed by focusing on the greenhouse gas emissions, energy consumption, biogas production and net energy. This procedure agrees with the procedure followed by Ref. [7]; where greenhouse gas emissions were further conceived by Ref. [13] as the foremost indices for the installation of biogas plants/units. Moreover, the function of the biogas system was considered to consist of two main parts, which are electricity generation and heat generation. This is practical and simulate the biogas plants/units and, therefore, this procedure disagrees with that followed by Ref. [5] which admirably supposed

that energy production is the only function of the biogas plant to let expanding the system boundaries to comprise additional functions for their investigation. The perception of this research was to implement a comparative life cycle analysis to assess the environ- mental impacts of implementing nanoparticles in the anaerobic digestion process to prove the avoided GHGs in CO2-equivalent, where the same perception was beforehand adopted by Ref. [4].

On the other hand, the results of the acidification, eutrophica- tion and human toxicity potential from electricity production through different biogas production scenarios showed that the use of cobalt nanoparticles causes the lowest negative environmental impact among all other variants. Further, the results of resource depletion and ozone layer depletion potential from electricity production through different biogas production scenarios showed that the use of cobalt nanoparticles is among the lowest values of negative environmental impact.

35: Various nanomaterials have been reported as additives during the anaerobic digestion for biogas production. Based on the results of this study, it can be concluded that reducing the greenhouse gas emissions can be achieved by using Co NPs (0.0225 kg CO2 eq./MJ elect.) during the biogas production process, whereas Ni NPs (0.0276 kg CO2 eq./MJ elect.), Fe NPs (0.0336 kg CO2 eq./MJ elect.), Fe3O4 NPs (0.0290 kg CO2 eq./MJ elect.) and the control (0.0366 kg CO2 eq./MJ elect.) caused higher greenhouse gas emissions than Co NPs (0.0225 kg CO2 eq./MJ elect.). Also, the addition of Co NPs had the lowest negative environmental impact in terms of acidification (1.499 X 10-5: kg SO2/MJ elect.), eutrophication (6.1 X 10-6 kg Phosphate eq./MJ elect.) and human toxicity potential (0.00218 kg DCB eq./MJ elect.) compared to Ni NPs, Fe NPs, Fe3O4 NPs and the control. However, Ni NPs had the lowest negative environmental impact in terms of resource depletion (0.056 MJ/MJ elect.) and ozone layer depletion potential kg R11 eq./MJ elect.). Accordingly, this study recommends the use of cobalt nanoparticles in the anaerobic digestion to avoid the negative impact on the environment resulted from the electricity generated using the produced biogas.
